## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe how nonverbal leakage lead to self-fulfilling prophecies

### You may have heard about a book called The Secret, which argues that you can send your positive thoughts out into the world, and positive things will come back to you. 

There's no evidence to support that idea, or even a theory for how it could possibly be true. 

> But nonverbal leakage, or the unintentional transmission of information via nonverbal behaviors, is real, and can lead to what is called a self-fulfilling prophecy. 

### Imagine a teacher has an implicit expectation that one child is a good student, and another child is a bad student. 

We can imagine a person schema for each student, one with the attribute smart, and one with the attribute ignorant. 

Though consciously the teacher tries to treat each child the same, nonverbal leakage may transmit very different information to each child. 

1. The so-called smart child will learn that the teacher has high expectations for him or her, 
2. whereas the so-called ignorant child will learn that the teacher has low expectations for him or her. 

### How does knowing that someone believes in you affect your behavior? 

Well, it makes you work harder and do better. 

### What about knowing that someone doesn't believe in you? 

Then you work less hard and perform worse. 

> That's the self-fulfilling prophecy. 

A teacher's expectation for each student may leak out via nonverbal leakage, and the students may respond in line with those expectations so that, at the end of the term, the so-called good students have better grades than the so-called bad students. 

### Let's try another example. 

This one has to do with job interviews. 

If you've ever been interviewed for a job, then you know it can be a nerve-wracking experience. 

You may also have had the experience that some interviewers 

* can set you at ease, 
* and others can ramp up your anxiety. 

### In which interview setting do you think you will perform better? 

> Researchers have found that white job interviewers have different expectations or stereotypes about how fit black and white job candidates are for the job. 

They have higher expectations for white job candidates than black job candidates. 

Though most job interviewers try to treat each job candidate equally, these racial expectations can leak out during the job interview and create a self-fulfilling prophecy. 

### In an initial study, Word, Zana, and Cooper found that white job interviewers treated Black and white job candidates very differently in terms of four nonverbal behaviors. 

1. The white job interviewers sat closer to the white job candidates, 
2. made more eye contact with the white job candidates, 
3. spent more time with them, 
4. and made fewer speech errors with white job candidates. 

> Together, these nonverbal behaviors are called immediacy behaviors, so we could say that the white job interviewers were more immediate with white job candidates than Black job candidates. 

So we know that the expectations of white job interviewers leaked out via immediacy behaviors during job interviews. 

### Here is where the self-fulfilling prophecy kicks in. 

> When we want someone to like us, we mimic them. 

In particular, we mimic their nonverbal behaviors. 

### So during a job interview, 

we tend to match the nonverbal behaviors of the job interviewers. This means the Black candidates could have: 

1. sat farther away, 
2. made less eye contact, 
3. had shorter interviews, 
4. and made more speech errors 

because they were trying to mimic the interviewer. 

### If that was the case, then they would likely have been judged as less qualified for the job because they were less immediate during the interview. 

A second study investigated this possibility. 

* In this study, white candidates, white Confederates, interviewed white job applicants, but treated half of them like the Black applicants were treated in the first study, 

> i.e. the Confederates sat farther away from them, made less eye contact, spent less time with them, and made more speech errors. 

The results were striking. 

The white participants treated like the Black candidates were in the first study were judged to be less qualified for the job. 

### Together, these two studies are a very convincing demonstration of the self-fulfilling prophecy in action. 

1. Job interviewer expectations about Black and white job candidates leak out via nonverbal behaviors related to immediacy. 
2. Job candidates mimic these nonverbal behaviors, 
3. and this leads to objectively worse interview performance for Black job candidates because they are mimicking the poor nonverbal behaviors of the interviewer.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe some of the methods researchers use to measure controlled processes

### Because people are consciously aware of their controlled processes, we can usually just ask them direct questions. 

* How do you feel about politician A? 
    * 1 to 7, with 1 meaning unfavourable and 7 meaning favourable. 
    
### But there are some types of questions that we can't ask people about directly because they are sensitive topics. 

When it comes to sensitive topics like race or sex, we can't just ask people directly because 

* people either don't want to tell us the truth, 
* or they don't know the truth. 

### Think about racism. 

If someone is racist and we directly ask them about their attitude toward Black people, they may not tell us the truth because of social norms that discourage acknowledging racism in public. 

> For example, feeling thermometers are a great way to measure attitudes. 

You ask participants to tell you how warm or favourable they feel toward a group of people, and they respond using the metaphor of the thermometer. 

These thermometers are a great way to assess *old-fashioned racism,* 

> a type of racism that people are proud of and want to tell everyone about. 

But you can also ask about this type of racism directly, with a question like, 

> "To what extent do you agree that Black people are not as smart as white people?" 

### But social norms may not allow people to honestly respond on this type of question. 

So researchers have come up with other strategies. 

* One of the most common involves a scale called the Modern Racism Scale. 

> The Modern Racism Scale asks participants about attitudes toward black people, but provides plausible, non-racist reasons for having a negative attitude toward black people. 

* For example, "To what extent do you agree that discrimination against black people is no longer a problem in America?" 

This type of question allows participants to acknowledge the problems of racism in the past, but to discount the effects of racism that are present now. 

> Another common strategy is to ask participants to tell us what they believe society thinks instead of what they personally believe. 

Because people tend to believe that their personal attitudes are commonly held, they end up telling us about their own attitudes. When they think about what most people think, they assume most people think like them. 

These types of questions solve the problem of the participant not wanting to tell us something, but they don't do anything for the situation in which participants don't know their true attitudes. 

### In those situations, we must rely on automatic measures.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe the shooter task
- Describe the effectiveness of training to reduce shooter bias among the police

### Let's talk about an automatic bias people may not know they have, the tendency to be quicker to shoot black people compared to white people. 

Unfortunately, Black people are a disproportionate amount of the victims of police killings, and sometimes it can seem that police are just quicker to shoot at Black people than they would be people of other races. 

### For example, police shot and killed a 12-year-old Black boy seconds after finding him playing at the park. 

Researchers have devised several ways of measuring these possible biases, one of which is called the shooter task. 

> The shooter task works by showing participants armed and unarmed targets and instructing them to make shoot and don't shoot decisions. 

Specifically, participants are shown four types of scenes. 

1. A black person with a gun, a shoot decision would be correct here. 
2. A black person without a gun, a don't shoot decision would be correct here. 
3. A white person with a gun, a shoot decision would be correct here. 
4. And a white person without a gun, a don't shoot decision would be correct here. 

### What researchers have consistently found is that when the suspect is armed, white participants are quicker to shoot black targets. 

But when the suspect is unarmed, white participants are quicker to make the don't shoot decision when the target is white. 

> In addition, participants make the most errors in the task when they have to make a decision about an unarmed Black target. 

In other words, the most common mistake is to shoot an unarmed Black person. 

The same effects are found among both undergraduate participants and community members, as well as national and local police officers. 

### However, there is another way of analyzing the data from this task which focuses on determining how confident a person needs to be before they decide to shoot a target. 

This measurement, called C for criterion, which is a form of bias, indicates how certain a person needs to feel that a target is armed before they shoot. 

* Local police officers and community members both need to feel significantly less certain that a Black person is armed before they decide to shoot. 
* However, national police officers do not show any bias against Black people on this measure. 

So if there is any good news here, let it be that the training national police officers receive seems to be effective at reducing this one form of bias. 

Perhaps we can convince local police departments to adopt whatever this training is. 

## Add these to your notes

1. White participants are quicker to shoot armed Black targets
2. White participants are slower to "don't shoot" unarmed Black targets
3. White participants make the most mistakes when the target is unarmed and Black (i.e., the most common mistake is to shoot unarmed Black people)
4. Community members are biased toward shooting Black and White targets but there is a stronger bias toward shooting Black targets
5. Local police are biased toward shooting Black targets and 'don't shooting' White targets
6. National police are biased toward "don't shooting" Black and White targets

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Define self-esteem
- Describe the logic of the IAT

### Do you have a favourite letter? 

Do you like some letters more than others? 

Researchers show that we tend to like the letters in our name more than other letters, especially our initials. 

> This is because we tend to like ourselves, and therefore we like anything linked to ourselves. 

We can imagine a self-schema with many positive attributes plus links from ourselves to our initials. 

### Interestingly, this effect extends to brands. 

If we share an initial with a brand, we're more likely to like that brand. 

> For example, my name is Curtis and I prefer Coke.

Although my last name is Phils, so maybe I should prefer Pepsi. 

### This effect also extends to group work in university classes. 

Have you ever been in a group that just didn't get along, or wasn't able to produce high quality work? 

Believe it or not, part of the reason for that may be because you and your group mates didn't share initials. 

> Research shows that groups which share initials perform better. 

### This effect also has implications for relationships. 

Imagine your self-schema with links from yourself in the centre to the important people in your life. 

> Researchers found that we preferred the initials of our romantic partners, parents, children, siblings, and friends. 

In fact, we also prefer to donate to disaster relief in the aftermath of hurricanes we share an initial with. 

So people with names starting with M were more likely to donate after Hurricane Matthew, and people with names starting with K were more likely to donate after Hurricane Katrina. 

> This name letter effect is one example of how researchers measure what is known as implicit self-esteem. 

### To understand why self-esteem is sometimes measured in this way, we need to think about the definition of self-esteem. 

> Now you may have heard other definitions in different classes, but in social psychology, a good definition of self-esteem is simply an evaluation of the self, or an attitude toward the self. 

We can imagine a self-schema with many different types of attributes, including relationships and initials, but most positive, but perhaps some negative. 

### An evaluation of the self can be thought of as whether most of those attributes are positive or most are negative. 

* The more positive traits there are, the more positive our evaluation of the self and the higher our self-esteem. 

### Another way to measure implicit self-esteem is with an Implicit Association Test, or IAT. 

> The self-esteem IAT is a double-categorization task that instructs participants to categorize words related to the self and words related to others, along with positive and negative concepts. 

There are five types of blocks or tasks during the test. 

1. In the first, participants practice categorizing self and other words. 
2. In the second, participants practice categorizing positive and negative concepts. 
3. The third block, however, is not for practice. This is one of the blocks researchers analyze. In this block, participants categorize self-related words and positive concepts using the same key, and use a different key to categorize other related words and negative concepts. 

For people that have a positive evaluation of their self, this double-categorization task should be easy because they can map the self and positive words to the same key in the brain. 

4. The fourth block is also a practice block in which participants learn to use the opposite keys to categorize positive and negative concepts. 
5. Finally, the fifth block is the other block that researchers analyze. In this block, participants categorize self-related words and negative concepts using the same key, and use a different key to categorize other related words and positive concepts. This should be a difficult task for participants that have a positive evaluation of the self, because they can no longer rely on the link in their brain between self and positive to make correct responses. 

> The logic behind the IAT is that implicit self-esteem can be thought of as how much faster participants are able to respond when the self and positive words share a response key compared to when the self and negative words share a response key. 

* A person with high self-esteem might respond on average in 400 milliseconds when the self and positive concepts share a key, but on average in 700 milliseconds when the self and negative concepts share a response key. 
* In contrast, a person with low self-esteem might respond on average in 600 milliseconds when the self and positive concepts share a response key, but on average in 500 milliseconds when the self and negative concepts share a response key. The name letter task and the IAT are the two most popular ways of measuring implicit self-esteem. 

* The most popular way to measure explicit self-esteem is just to ask a question like, 

> "To what extent do you feel that you were a person of worth?"

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- describe how together implicit and explicit self-esteem tell us more than either on their own

### In general, self-esteem is one of the most important constructs in all of psychology. 

It is generally a good thing. 

* It protects us from depression, drug abuse, even some types of delinquency. 

But it can also be bad. 

* Terrorists have high self-esteem, so do gang leaders. 

### When someone with high self-esteem is rejected, 

they can become ugly and abusive. 

So when you meet someone with high self-esteem, that could be a good thing or a bad thing. 

Someone with high explicit self-esteem might be:
* full of themselves and a narcissist, 
* or they might be a generally competent person. 

### How can you tell? 

> The answer may lie with implicit self-esteem. 

* Researchers have suggested that truly high self-esteem requires both high implicit and high explicit self-esteem. 
* Similarly, true low self-esteem requires both low implicit and low explicit self-esteem. 

### However, a person with high explicit self-esteem but low implicit self-esteem might best be described as having defensive self-esteem. 

This is the type of person who will tell you how good they are, but can't handle any criticism, and whom researchers have found tend to be narcissists. 

> So not everyone with high explicit self-esteem is a narcissist, just those people that have low implicit self-esteem. 

For narcissists then, they will tell you how great they are, but their self-schemas or their implicit self-evaluations will tell you a different story. 

### In fact, for many of these people, they don't even know that their implicit self-esteem is low. 

It turns out that we don't always know ourselves very well, which is why it is very important to measure both implicit and explicit self-esteem.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- define affective forecasting
- describe whether we mispredict our emotions in terms of type, intensity, and duration
- The relation between affective forecasting and racism

### How would you feel if you failed this course? 

Okay, now think about how you would feel in a happier situation. 

### What if you just won the lottery? 

Answering these types of questions are examples of *affective forecasting*, which refers to 

> predicting how we will feel in response to events in the future. 

Researchers found that people often mispredict how they will be emotionally influenced by events. 

We know: 
* which emotion we will feel, 
* we understand we will feel bad if we fail this course, 
* and we understand we will feel good if we win the lottery. 

> But we mispredict the intensity of the emotion. 

1. So yes, failing this course will feel bad, but actually not that bad. 
2. And winning the lottery will feel good, but not nearly as good as we tend to imagine it will. 

### In addition, we tend to mispredict the duration of the emotion. 

We think we will feel good or bad for much longer than we actually do. 

> We make these mistakes because when we are making the forecasts, we focus solely on the event and don't take into account the fact that we have a lot going on in our lives besides, say, failing this course. 

Also, we have a powerful psychological immune system which helps us get over traumatic events by employing strategies like 

1. rationalizing, 
2. discounting, 
3. and forgiving. 

### Let's try another example of affective forecasting. 

How would you feel if you were alone with another person in a waiting room and that person said something racist? 

If you're like most people, you imagine you would feel pretty bad. 

### That's what researchers found. 

* They asked participants to imagine waiting in a room with two other people, one Black and one white. 
* The Black person leaves the room and trips over the white person's legs, 
* and when the Black person is out of earshot, the white person turns to you and says either

1. "I hate it when black people do that," 
2. or, clumsy n-word, or 
3. makes no comment. 

> Participants that imagined hearing either racist's comments, either racist's comment forecasted that they would feel more negative emotional distress than participants that didn't imagine hearing the comment. 

### However, researchers also had a different group of participants actually experience this scenario. 

They actually waited in a room with a Black and white Confederate. 

* The Black Confederate really left a room 
* and really tripped over the white Confederate's legs, 
* and the white Confederate really either had no comment, said, "I hate it when black people do that," or, clumsy n-word. 

### In this real-life scenario, participants did not report more negative emotional distress after hearing either racist's comment compared to participants that didn't hear any comment. 

So though most participants imagined they would feel stressed out by a racist's comment, in reality, it had no impact at all. 

### Even worse, 

participants were asked who they would like to work with as a partner, the Black Confederate or the white confederate. 

> Once again, the racist's comment had no impact on partner choice. 

Participants were as willing to work with the white Confederate after the racist comments as they were after no comment. 

### If you find this result surprising, 

don't worry, most people do.

In fact, forecasters mostly predicted that they would not choose the white partner after hearing a racist comment. 

So even in situations in which we think it's obvious how we would feel, responding to clearly racist comments, we still mispredict both our emotions and our behavior.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- define attitudes

### Way back in 1935, 

Gordon Alport wrote that attitudes are *the single most indispensable construct in social psychology*. 

That view was reiterated in a book on attitudes by Brynjole and Petty in 2012. 

But they are also an indispensable part of our everyday language. 

### Let me give you an example. 

If I told you that John has a bad attitude, you would know right away what I meant. 

* John is motivated, 
* John is mean, 
* John is generally someone to stay away from. 

### But we can also talk about other types of attitudes. 

We tend to automatically form an inference about a person's attitude just from their appearance. 

> And that attitude tells us a lot of information. 

* Is this someone we should approach? 
* Should we steer clear of this individual? 
* Might we want to spend time with them? 

And so, without even getting into the social psychological definition of attitudes, we can already see why they are so important. 

### We believe they tell us something about how someone is likely to behave. 

In fact, that is the reason Alport and other researchers have called attitudes so indispensable to social psychologists. 

> Measuring attitudes is often thought to be a great way to predict someone's behavior. 

And, as I'm sure you'll remember from the module on attribution theory, understanding the behavior of other people is something that we like to spend a lot of time trying to do. 

> The definition of attitudes that we will use in this class is an evaluation of a person, object, or idea. 

### If you remember, our definition of self-esteem was very similar, an evaluation of the self. 

So, in this class, we can think about self-esteem as an attitude, but instead of being an attitude towards something else or something external to us, it is an attitude toward ourselves, and it should tell us something about how we treat ourselves.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- define attitudes in multiple ways

### Today, when we talk about attitudes and social psychology, we are talking about evaluations. 

On the previous page, I suggested that attitudes or evaluations are important because they help us predict behavior and research has actually shown us that they provide valuable information about a variety of processes, including 

* how we visually process others, 
* how we make the decision to vote, 
* how we choose our friends, 
* and even how we decide what to buy. 

### In addition, implicit attitudes or automatic evaluations predict spontaneous or nonverbal behaviors during interracial interactions. 

Now, though most early definitions described attitudes in terms of how they were thought to influence behaviors, other definitions have actually included behavior in the definition.

And in fact, one of the first uses of the word attitude in psychology was in reference to a behavior. In the 1800s, Galton defined attitudes as 

 > bodily inclination toward or away from someone. 

He believed he could determine how much people liked one another at a dinner party by observing how they leaned toward or away from one another. 

### The famous Tripartite Theory of Attitudes by Rosenberg and Hovland also argues that behavior is one component of attitudes, along with cognitions and affect. 

More recently, Eagly and Chaikin argued in the 1990s that 

> attitudes consist of an evaluative and a behavioral component. 

Attitudes are expected to positively correlate with behavior such that 

1. positive attitudes should be related to behaviors that approach, support, or enhance the attitude object, and 
2. negative attitudes should be related to behaviors that avoid, oppose, or hinder the object. 

### However, in 1928, 

the psychologist Thurstone published an article arguing that attitudes could be measured by asking people their 

> opinions or evaluations of people, objects, or ideas. 

And from there, the evaluative component of attitudes and the idea of these evaluations as attitudes that could predict behavior began to take center stage. 

### But the relationship between attitudes and behavior isn't so simple. 

A classic example of the complex relationship between attitudes and behavior comes from an early study by Lapierre, who in 1934 examined the attitudes and subsequent behavior of hotel proprietors toward Chinese people. 

> Although 92% of hotel proprietors who returned a mailed questionnaire indicated that they would refuse service to potential Chinese customers, only one out of a sample of 250 actually did refuse the business of a visiting Chinese couple. 

Thus, even strongly expressed negative attitudes may not be predictive of behavior. 

### In fact, by 1969, 

a meta-analysis by Wicker found there was little evidence that attitudes were strong predictors of behaviors. 

So although attitudes are important because they predict behavior, the story isn't always so simple.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- describe three methods for measuring explicit attitudes
- state two reasons we cannot always only ask about explicit attitudes

### Using a dual process model framework, 

explicit methods of measuring attitudes allow participants to use their controlled processes to respond. 

Going all the way back to Thurstone in 1928, what is still one of the most popular ways to measure attitudes is to directly ask participants. 

* For example, we could directly ask people their opinion about food, sports, movies, people, social categories, etc. 
* Another method we have also previously discussed is to use feeling thermometers. People simply indicate how warm or favourable they feel toward an attitude object, which could be a person, object, or idea. 
* A third method for directly asking about attitudes is to use what is called a semantic differential. The way I remember this type of scale is to recall that semantic refers to words, and differential is some sort of comparison. So a semantic differential scale involves two opposing words. 

> Do you believe Black people are closer to good or bad, pleasant or unpleasant, etc. 

### However, sometimes you can't ask a person's attitude directly because participants will tell you more than they know. 

For example, do you agree or disagree with the Attorney General's interpretation of the 15th Amendment? 

You can answer that question, but that doesn't mean you actually know either 

* what the 15th Amendment is, 
* or what the AG's interpretation of it is, 
* and what your attitude is toward that. 

But you can still give an answer. 

So it's really important for researchers not to ask questions that force participants to give answers they don't know. 

### On the other hand, there are times where you do know the truth, but you don't want to tell it. 

In this case, we try to ask indirectly. This is the case for what we call "sensitive attitudes." These are attitudes toward 

* social categories, 
* religion, 
* sex, 
* political parties. 

Consider these direct attempts at asking a participant's attitude or evaluation of Black people. 
1. What is your evaluation of Black people? 1 to 7. 
2. How warm or favorable do you feel toward Black people? 
3. How good or bad are Black people?  

All are great ways to directly ask the question, but social norms may prevent a person from answering honestly. 

Therefore, Social psychologists have developed indirect questions. 

For example, here is a sample question from what's called the Modern Racism Scale. 

1. The government should not help make any special effort to help Black people because they should help themselves. 

This question doesn't directly ask about your evaluation of Black people, and provides a socially acceptable reason for responding in a manner that is negative toward Black people. 

That 'socially acceptable' reason is conservative political ideology.

So an indirect method of measuring attitudes can sometimes be more accurate.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe the theory of planned behavior
- Understand how strong vs weak and general vs specific attitudes are related to behavior

### What predicts planned behavior? 

If you think back to the page on dual process models, the word *planned* is important here. 

It means we are dealing with controlled or deliberate processes. 

The aptly named *theory of planned behavior* argues that attitudes actually aren't the best predictors of behavior. 

### Behavioral intentions are. 

To understand why this might be the case, let's think about my plan to decide whether to recycle a can of cherry coke or not. 

My attitude toward recycling is my evaluation of recycling or how warm I feel toward recycling. That definitely sounds like it should predict whether I recycle. 

> But behavioral intentions are conscious decisions that carry out specific actions. 

So the theory of planned behavior argues that my conscious decision to recycle this can of cherry coke is the best predictor of whether I actually will recycle it. 

### And that makes a lot of sense. 

But it also pushes the question about what predicts behavior back one step. Instead of asking what predicts behavior, we now have a new question. 

> What predicts behavioral intentions? 

The theory of planned behavior argues three things predict behavioral intentions. 

1. The first is, of course, my attitude toward recycling this can of cherry coke. 
2. The second is norms around recycling. If I'm in a situation where recycling is encouraged, then I'm more likely to recycle. 
3. And the third is something called behavioral control. You can think of this construct as how easy it is for me to recycle. 

* Imagine I'm in my office and I just finished this delicious can of cherry coke. I have a garbage can in my office, but to recycle, I have to walk down the hall and around the corner.* In this situation, the behavioral control is low*. 
* Compare that to this situation. I have a garbage can and a blue box in my office. Now behavioral control is high. 
* Or you could even imagine a third option, which I have only a blue box in my office, but not a garbage can. Here, behavioral control would be extremely high. 

### To sum up, the theory of planned behavior says that behavioral intentions are the best predictor of behavior, 

and the best predictor of behavioral intentions are 

1. specific attitudes, 
2. social norms, and 
3. behavioral control. 

But let's take a step back and think about what I mean by *specific* attitudes, as opposed to just attitudes. 

### Imagine the behavior I'm interested in is whether someone has used the birth control in the past two years. 

Which attitude do you think will be the best predictor of that? 

* Their attitude toward birth control? 
* Their attitude toward birth control pills? 
* Their attitude toward *using* birth control pills? 

You can see that these questions become more and more specific. 

> And it turns out, the more specific the attitude, the better it is at predicting a specific behavior. 

On the other hand, 

> a general attitude is predicting behaviors in aggregate. 

So a general attitude toward religion won't be a great predictor of whether you will attend religious services this week. 

A specific attitude, or a behavioral intention, is the best predictor of that specific behavior. 

> But a general attitude will be a better predictor of a wide array of religious behaviors over time. 

Attending services, reading holy books, etc. 

The final thing I will say about explicit attitudes and deliberate behaviors is that *strong* attitudes are better predictors than *weak* attitudes. 

When you have a strong attitude, that is often because 
1. you have more information about the attitude object, 
2. or you are personally involved with the attitude object, 
3. or you have direct experience with the attitude object, and that strong attitude is brought to mind more easily. 

### It is more accessible. 

So when Wicker, back in 1969, argued there wasn't a lot of evidence that attitudes are strong predictors of behavior, he was right. 

But since then, researchers have done a great job of understanding what does predict our *planned* behaviors.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe which behaviors implicit attitudes are better predictors of
- Describe the MODE model

### The Implicit Association Test is by far the most popular way to measure automatic responses, 

and it is not without controversy among researchers and non-researchers alike. 

When you took the IAT, you may have found yourself developing arguments against it being a valid measure of attitudes. 

However, one argument in favor of a measure of attitudes is always just, 

> "Does it predict behavior?" 

A meta-analysis in 2009 asked that question by conducting a meta-analysis of 122 research reports. 

They compared how well the IAT predicted behavior and compared that to how well self-report, or explicit measures of attitudes, predicted behavior. 

They found implicit measures of sensitive attitudes, like attitudes toward social categories like Black people, were better predictors of behavior toward Black people than self-report measures. 

In my opinion, that alone is a good argument for the value of the IAT. 

### However, when it comes to 

* political preferences, 
* alcohol and drug use, 
* phobias, anxieties, and 
* personality traits, 

self-reports were better predictors of behavior. 

It is important to note, though, that in regards to those situations, 

> using both the IAT and self-reports was the best strategy. 

Both types of measurements provided unique information or predictive power for the behavior. 

So again, we have a good argument for the value of the IAT, but let's take a closer look at how strong a predictor the IAT is of intergroup behavior. 

### When Wicker did his meta-analysis in 1969, 

he suggested that a correlation of r = 0.15 between attitudes and behavior wasn't good evidence that attitudes were strong predictors of behavior. 

Now, if you look at the size of the correlation between IAT scores and intergroup behavior, you see it isn't much stronger than that. 

### Subsequent meta-analyses have actually suggested that this correlation may actually be slightly smaller, about where Wicker found it to be, in the teens. 

And that's where we are in the field right now. 

> We have a weak correlation between implicit attitudes and intergroup behavior. 

* Does that mean that implicit processes are only weak predictors of behavior? 
* Or, like 50 years ago, will researchers discover a number of other factors that, when taken into account, strengthen the attitude-behavior link? 

There is some reason to think that the story about implicit responses and behavior isn't complete yet. 

### The MODE model brings a dual-process approach to this area. 

MODE stands for 

> Motivation and Opportunity as Determinants of Whether Behavior is Related to Spontaneous or Deliberate Processes. 

In the language of this course, spontaneous processes are automatic processes, and deliberate processes are controlled processes. 

The gist of this theory is that, 

* if you are motivated and can control a particular behavior, then controlled processes or self-reports should be a better predictor. 
* But if you lack either the motivation or the ability to control the behavior, then automatic processes should be a better predictor. 

Which is a long way of saying that 

> implicit responses should be related to non-verbal behaviors, and explicit responses should be related to deliberate behaviors. 

Dovidio and colleagues provided support for this theory in 2002. 

* They assessed the attitudes of white participants toward Black people using implicit and explicit measures, 
* and then observed those participants while they interacted with a Black partner. 

They found that 

1. implicit measures of attitudes were significant predictors of non-verbal behavior, 
2. but explicit measures of attitudes were significant predictors of deliberate behavior. 

So if I was to make a prediction, I would suggest that the next 10 to 15 years will feature a lot of research designed to understand the relationship between implicit measures of attitudes and behavior.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe how the principle of self-presentation influences attitudes

### There have been several definitions of attitudes that included a behavioral component. 

From that perspective, it doesn't make sense to focus exclusively on how attitudes may predict behavior. 

> We also need to consider the possibility that behaviors can predict attitudes. 

### Imagine you and a friend, 

let's call this friend Jeff, are walking to class and you are approached by a person soliciting donations for a charity about hurricane relief. 

You find their appeal persuasive so you donate $5. 

Later, a different friend asks about your attitude toward hurricane relief charity. 

### How will you answer? 

Do you think your answer will be influenced by whether your friend Jeff is around? 

> The principle of self-presentation suggests that it does. 

* If your friend Jeff, who saw you give the money to the charity, is there, then self-presentation principle suggests that you will report a more positive attitude toward the charity. 
* But if no one who saw you donate to the charity is present, then you will report a less positive attitude toward the charity. 

> You can think of this as the "I don't want to look like a hypocrite" principle. 

You want to avoid your friend Jeff speaking up and saying something like, "Are you sure you're only lukewarm toward that charity? I just saw you donate $5." 

But it is important to note that 

> this is not the "I don't want to BE a hypocrite" principle. It is the "I don't want to LOOK like a hypocrite" principle. 

It doesn't change your privately held or privately reported attitudes, and it only takes place when you are consciously aware of a possible discrepancy between your behavior and your attitude. 

So, thinking about dual process models, self-presentation is related to *controlled* processes.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Define cognitive dissonance
- Define insufficient justification
- Define post-decisional dissonance

### Have you ever taken a class where the professor required you to complete several hours of psychology experiments through SONA? 

If you have, then I bet there is a good chance that at least one of those experiments was fairly boring. 

* Imagine that at the end of this boring experiment, you were told the experiment is actually about how being told an experiment is fun or boring influences how enjoyable the experiment is. 
* And it turns out you were in the control condition because no one told you whether the experiment was fun or boring at the beginning. 
* However, the experimenters would like you to tell the next participant that the experiment is fun. 

> This creates what is known as dissonance, a tension that arises from knowing that two of your cognitions are inconsistent with each other. 

In this case, your knowledge of the experiment is boring and your action telling the next participant that the experiment is fun. 

After you tell the lie, the experimenter then pays you 

* $1, 
* $20 or 
* nothing. 

And here is where the real experiment takes place: You are asked how much you personally enjoyed the boring experiment. 

### Which participants do you think enjoyed the experiment the most? 

To answer that question, you need to think about which participants would experience the most dissonance because the more dissonance a participant feels, the more they will change their attitudes to match their behavior. 

* The participants that weren't paid any money experienced very little dissonance. They can justify a lie by telling themselves that they were just doing what the experimenter asked them to do. 
* Also, the participants that were paid $20 experienced very little dissonance. They can justify a lie by appealing to the $20. 
* But the participants that were only paid $1 experienced a large amount of dissonance. They don't have enough justification for their lie. $1 isn't enough. They claim to have enjoyed the experiment the most and this is real attitude change. They really feel like they enjoyed the experiment more than other participants did. 

### This experiment demonstrates one principle by which cognitive dissonance can change attitudes. Insufficient justification. 

> The principle of insufficient justification states that we change our attitudes to be more consistent with our behaviors if we do not have a strong justification for acting contrary to our attitudes. 

If we do have a reasonable justification, we will not change our attitudes. 

### There is another principle by which cognitive dissonance or self-justification can change attitudes. 

Have you ever been in a situation like this? 

* You saved up $15 to buy a CD at HMV but there were two CDs you really want to buy? 

### No? 

Okay. 

* You saved up $20 to buy a DVD at Walmart but there were two DVDs you really want to buy?

### Still No? 

Okay, um, you saved up enough money to buy a new phone. But there were two phones you really want? 

### In this situation, you would probably feel a lot of dissonance. 

So what do you do? 

Fortunately, researchers have studied this scenario. 

* Imagine you were a participant and you were asked to rate the attractiveness of a number of different posters. 
* Afterward, the experimenters show you two of the posters that you rated very positively and they tell you that you can pick one of them to take home. 
* Now, you should be feeling dissonance just like you would with the two phones. Well, fortunately, dissonance leads to attitude change. 

> So participants change their attitudes so that they like the poster they chose more. 

hat's great news for the next time you have to choose between two new phones. 

> Your brain will use what is called post-decisional dissonance to make you like the chosen phone more. 

In other conditions, the researchers asked participants to choose between two disliked posters. 

> Those participants ended up disliking the rejected poster more. 

So this video has described two ways that behavior can change attitudes through cognitive dissonance. 

1. The first is due to insufficient justification. If we can justify our inconsistent behavior, then we need to change our attitudes. 
2. The second is due to post-decisional dissonance. We change our attitudes so that we like the chosen items more and the rejected items less.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

### Remember telling more than you know? 

This is the type of situation in which an experimenter has asked you a question that you don't know the answer to, but you come up with an answer anyway. 

Well, I'm going to talk about one way we come up with those answers, *self-perception*. 

* Let's imagine someone asked me how much I like the show Black-ish. 
* I would think about how often I've watched the show and realize I've seen every episode. 
* And, I'm not just watching because my significant other or other people in my family like it. 
* So I conclude that I really do like it. 

> But if I only watched Black-ish because my son likes to watch it, then I wouldn't conclude I really like it. 

So if I don't have another justification for the behavior, then I change my attitude to match it. 

This is a *controlled* process. 

But behavior can influence our attitudes without us even noticing. 

Now, here's another way our self-perceptions can influence our attitudes. 

Try this. 

* Hold a pen in your lips. 

Got it? 

* Now read this comic:

Now try this. 

* Hold a pen in your teeth. 

Got it? 

* Now read this comic:

### Which comic was funnier to you? 

> According to research, people will tend to find the second comic funnier, because while they read it, the muscles around their mouths were in a smiling position. 

There is a close relationship between *bodily feedback and higher cognitions*. 

* If we're smiling, our brains take that as a signal that something is funny. 

> This is called social embodiment. 

It is the idea that our actions and body movements can directly influence our thoughts and attitudes. 

Importantly, these social embodiment effects take place without our conscious awareness. 

* They are an automatic process. 

A similar effect happens if we are nodding or shaking our head. 

If a researcher asks you to place a pair of headphones on and test how comfortable they are by nodding or shaking your head, you may be in a social embodiment experiment. 

* Imagine you were nodding your head like the experimenter asked you to, and you were presented with a pen. 
* The experimenter asks you, "Do you prefer this pen, or do you want another pen?" 
* Researchers found that, while nodding your head, you preferred the pen presented to you, because you were already making the body movements associated with saying "yes." 
* But if you were shaking your head, then you would prefer a different pen, because you were already making the body movements associated with saying "no." 

Social embodiment doesn't just work for head movements, though. 

> Arm movements have a similar effect. 

1. What arm muscles are used when you pull someone towards yourself and hug them? 
2. What about when you push someone away? 

> It turns out that arm flexion, the muscles you move when you pull something towards yourself, makes you like them more. 

> And arm extension, the muscles you use when you push someone away from yourself, makes you like things less. 

So you don't just hug someone because you like them. You like them because you hugged them.

2. Describe how social embodiment changes attitudes
