questions.prompt: Which statement best describes the practical use of Dr. Phills' statistical power rules of thumb?
answers: They help determine whether a study had enough participants to detect the effect the researchers were interested in
They prove the correct statistical test was used
They guarantee groups are identical in every way
They show whether a study has high external validity
questions.explanation: These rules can be used to judge whether a study had enough power or enough participants to detect the effect of interest.

questions.prompt: What is a reason a p-value might not be valid?
answers: The study used too many participants
The researchers ran the wrong statistical test
The theory was too broad
The p-value is too high
questions.explanation: A p-value isn't valid if esearchers  have run the wrong statistical test for the question they were asking. Also, whether a p-value is high or low is irrelevant to the validity of the p-value

questions.prompt: Why should p-values based on small samples not be trusted?
answers: Small samples automatically make the null hypothesis false
Small samples make it impossible to compute a p-value
With small samples each new observation can change the p-value a lot
Small samples are generalizable
questions.explanation: When a sample is small, each additional observation adds a lot of information proportionally, so the p-value may shift substantially. Generalizability is related to external validity and random sampling (Who is in the sample, not how many people are in the sample)

questions.prompt: What happens to Type 1 error rates when researchers repeatedly stop and calculate a p-value during data collection?
answers: It decreases to nearly zero
It stays exactly the same
It increases
It becomes the same as statistical power
questions.explanation: Every time researchers calculate the p-value during data collection, they increase the Type 1 error rate.

questions.prompt: What is one purpose of a preregistration document?
answers: To let researchers revise their hypotheses after seeing the data
To publicly declare a stopping rule before the study is run
To guarantee the p-value will be below 0.05
To increase the number of participants
questions.explanation: Preregistration lets researchers publicly declare in advance when data collection will stop, such as after a certain number of participants or on a certain date. This provides proof that the researchers didn't keep collecting data until their p-value was low enough.

questions.prompt: What does internal validity refer to?
answers: Whether findings generalize to other settings
Confidence that the manipulation of the IV is responsible for changes in the DV
How well a measure captures a construct
Whether participants were randomly sampled
questions.explanation: Internal validity is our confidence that the manipulation of the independent variable is truly responsible for observed changes in the dependent variable. We want to make sure that only difference between groups is the one thing we are manipulating.

questions.prompt: What kind of variable weakens internal validity when it varies along with the independent variable?
answers: A mediator
A dependent variable
A confounding variable
A random variable
questions.explanation: Internal validity is weakened when another variable varies along with the independent variable, creating a confound.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains how a confounding variable weakens internal validity?
answers: It makes it harder to know whether the IV or another variable caused changes in the DV
It guarantees the null hypothesis will be rejected
It increases the number of participants in the study
It ensures the measure has construct validity
questions.explanation: A confound weakens internal validity because it prevents researchers from confidently attributing changes in the DV to the IV.

questions.prompt: Suppose that I conduct an experiment on the effects of flattery on compliance. I bring women into the laboratory, tell them they are beautiful, and ask them to lend me $10. I bring men into the laboratory, I don't flatter them, and I ask them to lend me $10. Sure enough, more women than men lend me $10. My conclusion that flattery increases compliance is suspect because
answers: I failed to randomly select the sample from the population.
my experiment lacks external validity.
my experiment lacks internal validity.
I have not operationally defined my variables.
questions.explanation: A confound weakens internal validity because it prevents researchers from confidently attributing changes in the DV to the IV.

questions.prompt: What does external validity refer to?
answers: Confidence that the IV caused changes in the DV
The extent to which a study's findings generalize to other settings conditions and populations
Whether a measure captures the intended construct
Whether researchers used the correct statistical test
questions.explanation: External validity is the extent that a study's findings generalize to other settings, conditions, and populations.

questions.prompt: Why might a study with only women participants  not automatically be described as having poor external validity?
answers: Because women are always representative of all people
Because the study has perfect internal validity
Because we do not yet know whether the findings generalize to men
Because external validity does not apply to gender
questions.explanation: In this case the more accurate statement is that we do not know whether the findings generalize to men.

questions.prompt: What is social cognition?
answers: The study of how people are biologically wired for social behavior
Applying what we know about learning and memory to how we learn about and remember people
The study of group decision making across cultures
The statistical analysis of stereotypes
questions.explanation: Social cognition as applying what we know about learning and memory to the study of how we learn about and remember people.

questions.prompt: What was social cognition originally called?
answers: Trait inference
Schema theory
Person-memory
Social perception
questions.explanation: Social cognition was originally called person-memory.

questions.prompt: Which set of three questions are central to social cognition?
answers: How we observe, imitate, and reward persons
How we encode, store, and retrieve information about persons
How we sample, randomize, and analyze persons
How we like, dislike, and avoid persons
questions.explanation: The lesson emphasizes three core issues: how we encode, store, and retrieve information about persons.

questions.prompt: What is a schema?
answers: A statistical rule for analyzing social behavior
A mental structure that represents knowledge about a concept or type of stimulus
A temporary emotional reaction to another person
A method for randomly assigning participants to groups
questions.explanation: Schemas are mental structures that represent knowledge about a concept or type of stimulus. There are many types of schemas (role, person, event, etc.)

questions.prompt: Which of the following is one of the rules for using schemas?
answers: Use the most complex schema available
Use the most negative schema available
Use a schema that is relevant
Use a schema that was learned first in childhood
questions.explanation: People use  schemas that are relevant.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is one of the rules for using schemas?
answers: Use a schema that is salient
Use a schema that has the most attributes
Use a schema only if it is self-related
Use a schema that has been experimentally tested
questions.explanation: People use schemas that are salient, accessible, or active.

questions.prompt: ________ are the cognitive structures we use to organize our knowledge of the social world.
answers: Social roles
Schemas
Heuristics
Social cognitions
questions.explanation: Schemas are mental structures that represent knowledge about a concept or type of stimulus. There are many types of schemas (role, person, event, etc.)

questions.prompt: What is attribution theory?
answers: The scientific study of how people remember social events
The scientific study of how we infer the causes of other people's behavior
The scientific study of how schemas become stereotypes
The scientific study of how attitudes change over time
questions.explanation: Attribution theory is the scientific study of how we infer the causes of other people's behavior. To what do we attribute their behavior?

questions.prompt: What core question does attribution theory ask?
answers: How accurately can people recall a conversation?
Why is this person doing this?
How large is the effect size in the study?
Which attributes are useful?
questions.explanation: Attribution theory asks why people are doing something and what is causing their behavior.

questions.prompt: What is an internal attribution?
answers: Inferring that behavior is caused by something internal such as an attitude character or personality
Inferring that behavior is caused entirely by the physical environment
Inferring that behavior is random and has no cause
Inferring that behavior is caused by internal validity
questions.explanation: An internal attribution is inferring that a person's behavior is caused by something internal, such as a personal attitude, character, or personality.

questions.prompt: What is an external attribution?
answers: Inferring that behavior is caused by a personal trait
Inferring that behavior is caused by something external such as the situation
Inferring that behavior is caused by biology alone
Inferring that behavior is caused by external validity
questions.explanation: An external attribution as inferring that a person's behavior is caused by something external, like the situation.

questions.prompt: Which option best distinguishes internal from external attributions?
answers: Internal focuses on the situation and external focuses on attitude
Internal focuses on personality or attitude and external focuses on situational causes
Internal and external attributions mean the same thing
Internal focuses on group behavior and external focuses on individual behavior
questions.explanation: Internal attributions as caused by personal attitudes, character, or personality whereas external attributions as caused by the situation.

questions.prompt: What is the fundamental attribution error?
answers: The tendency to overestimate situational causes of other people's behavior
The tendency to make internal attributions for others' behavior
The tendency to avoid making any attribution
The tendency to confuse schemas with stereotypes
questions.explanation: The fundamental attribution error is the tendency to make internal attributions to the causes of others' behavior even when it is clear that situational factors are involved.

questions.prompt: What is the discounting principle?
answers: When behavior is driven by the situation it does not tell us about who a person really is
All behavior should be explained by personality first
Behavior is a  reflection of  attitudes
Situational factors matter only when everyone acts the same way
questions.explanation: The discounting principle states that when behavior is driven by the situation, behavior does not tell us anything about who a person really is.

questions.prompt: What is the Lewinian equation?
answers: Behavior is caused by a person's disposition
Behavior is caused by situational pressure
Behavior is the combination of situational pressures and a person's disposition
Behavior is related to attributions
questions.explanation: The Lewinian equation defines a person's behavior as the combination of situational pressures or external factors and a person's disposition or internal attributes.

questions.prompt: When does consensus support an external attribution?
answers: When everyone behaves the same way in the same situation
When one person behaves the same way in every situation
When a person's behavior matches their self-schema
When participants were free to choose their position
questions.explanation: If everyone is acting the same way in a situation, there is consensus, which is good evidence that the situation is shaping behavior.

questions.prompt: When does consistency support an internal attribution?
answers: When everyone in the room behaves similarly
When a person always behaves the same way across multiple situations
When a person is responding to a strong situational demand
When behavior is clearly non-diagnostic
questions.explanation: If someone acts the same way across multiple situations, we have consistency and good reason to make an internal attribution.

questions.prompt: Which example best illustrates the consistency principle?
answers: Everyone in a classroom is quiet
A debater argues a side they were assigned
A person is loud at a party in a classroom and on the bus
A salesperson praises a car they are trying to sell
questions.explanation: The consistency principle applies when a person acts similarly in many different situations

questions.prompt: What do observers tend to focus on when making attributions about other people's behavior?
answers: The person's behavior
Their own past experiences
Random chance
The demands of the situation
questions.explanation: Observers focus on the person's behavior, which is the salient information for them.

questions.prompt: What do actors tend to focus on when making attributions about their own behavior?
answers: Their personality
The situation
Stereotypes
Group membership
questions.explanation: When we are the actor, we are looking out into the world, so the situation becomes the salient information.

questions.prompt: What is perceptual salience?
answers: The tendency to notice only positive behavior
The idea that whatever we are focused on is what we use to make the attribution
The assumption that all behavior reflects true character
The tendency to remember behavior better than situations
questions.explanation: Perceptual salience means that whatever is most perceptually salient to us, such as behavior or the situation, is what we use to make the attribution.

questions.prompt: Which attribution pattern best reflects self-serving bias?
answers: Internal attributions for personal failures and external attributions for personal successes
External attributions for both personal successes and failures
Internal attributions for personal successes and external attributions for personal failures
Internal attributions for everyone regardless of outcome
questions.explanation: People tend to explain their own successes internally and their own failures externally in order to maintain a positive view of themselves.

questions.prompt: Why do people often make internal attributions when they do well on a test?
answers: Because they want to see themselves as intelligent
Because tests measure ability
Because success is caused by personal traits
Because the situation is more salient than behavior
questions.explanation: People make internal attributions for their own good performance because they want to believe they are intelligent.

questions.prompt: Why do people often make external attributions when they do poorly on a test?
answers: Because they want to protect a positive view of themselves
Because poor performance always has situational causes
Because intelligence is not important
Because observers force them to
questions.explanation: People often attribute poor performance to an unfair or difficult test because they want to preserve a positive self-image.

questions.prompt: What is the ultimate attribution error ?
answers: Making attributions about in-group and out-group members in ways that make the in-group look good and out-groups look bad
Always making internal attributions for all behavior
Ignoring consensus and consistency when studying social groups
Making attributions about in-group and out-group members in ways that make the in-group look good but not the out-group look bad
questions.explanation: The ultimate attribution error is making attributions about in-group and out-group member behaviors in ways that favor the in-group and disadvantage the out-group.

questions.prompt: What attribution do people tend to make when an in-group member does something good?
answers: An external attribution
An internal attribution
No attribution at all
A random attribution
questions.explanation: When an in-group member does something good, people tend to make an internal attribution.

questions.prompt: What attribution do people tend to make when an out-group member does something good?
answers: An internal attribution
A mixed attribution based on consistency
An external attribution
No attribution at all
questions.explanation: When an out-group member does something good, people tend to make an external attribution so that their own group still looks superior.

questions.prompt: What is culture?
answers: A set of temporary preferences shared by a small group
The enduring behaviors ideas attitudes and traditions shared by a large group and transmitted across generations
A collection of laws created by governments
Art, music, movies, and poetry
questions.explanation: Culture is the enduring behaviors, ideas, attitudes, and traditions shared by a large group of people and transmitted from one generation to the next.

questions.prompt: What does individualism promote?
answers: Priority for group goals and identity based on relationships
Priority for one's own goals and identity defined by personal attributes
Holistic reasoning and acceptance of contradiction
Defining the self mainly through extended family roles
questions.explanation: Individualism is giving priority to one's own goals over group goals and defining identity in terms of personal attributes.

questions.prompt: What does collectivism promote?
answers: Priority for personal achievement over family and work groups
Priority for group goals and identity defined through connections to others
Ignoring context and focusing only on the focal object
Analytic reasoning that rejects contradiction
questions.explanation: Collectivism is giving priority to the goals of one's group and defining identity accordingly.

questions.prompt: Which pair correctly matches the reasoning styles?
answers: Individualism with holistic reasoning and collectivism with analytic reasoning
Individualism with analytic reasoning and collectivism with holistic reasoning
Both individualism and collectivism with analytic reasoning
Both individualism and collectivism with holistic reasoning
questions.explanation: Individualism prioritizes analytic reasoning, whereas collectivism uses holistic reasoning that considers multiple influences.

questions.prompt: What do dual process models propose?
answers: There is only one stable way people think about others
There are two modes of thinking-one quick and one slow
There are two modes of thinking-one conscious and one intentional
People always make external attributions first
questions.explanation: Dual process models include two modes of thinking, one that is quick and easy to use and one that takes longer and more effort.

questions.prompt: Which pair of labels best describe a dual-process distinction?
answers: Random versus nonrandom
Internal versus external
Automatic versus controlled
Reliable versus unreliable
questions.explanation: Thinking fast versus thinking; slow, or automatic versus controlled;, or implicit versus explicit;, or even conscious versus non-conscious.

questions.prompt: What happens in the third stage of the three-stage model of attribution when forming impressions of others?
answers: People automatically make an internal attribution
People effortfully make an external attribution
People effortfully make an internal attribution
People automatically make an external attribution
questions.explanation: The third stage is making an external attribution, and  this is an effortful process.

questions.prompt: New professors often have to concentrate very hard to give a lecture, and monitor themselves carefully to make sure they're not talking too fast and that they are using appropriate and clear examples. More seasoned professors don't have to concentrate as much, and can talk clearly and engagingly, reorder the overheads, and keep an eye on students all at the same time. This example represents the transition from _______ processing to _______ processing as professors gain experience.
answers: controlled; automatic
random; systematic
automatic; controlled
unconscious; conscious
questions.explanation: When a task is new, it takes effort to complete. But with practice, it can be complete via automatic processes

questions.prompt: What is the availability heuristic?
answers: Assessing frequency or likelihood by how easily examples come to mind
Assessing likelihood by how random something looks
Assessing value by comparing pros and cons
Assessing people based on group membership
questions.explanation: The availability heuristic is the tendency to assess frequency, likelihood, or probability based on how easily something can be remembered or brought to mind.

questions.prompt: Why do people often mistakenly think words ending in -ing are more common?
answers: Because -ing words are objectively more numerous than words ending in n and g
Because it is easier to think of examples of -ing words, which triggers the availability heuristic
Because English grammar requires more -ing words
Because it is easier to think of examples of -ing words, which triggers the representativeness heuristic
questions.explanation: People often judge -ing words as more common because such examples come to mind easily, illustrating the availability heuristic.

questions.prompt: What is the representative heuristic?
answers: Basing judgments on an initial number and adjusting from it
Assessing likelihood based on stereotypes or expectations of similar occurrences
Remembering dramatic events better than ordinary ones
Making choices by focusing only on goals
questions.explanation: The representative heuristic as the tendency to assess the likelihood of an event based on stereotypes or expectations of similar occurrences.

questions.prompt: What is the anchoring and adjustment heuristic?
answers: The tendency to favor information that confirms prior beliefs
The tendency to be influenced by an initial value and then adjust from it
The tendency to judge frequency by memory ease
The tendency to assume events with similar features have similar causes
questions.explanation: Anchoring and adjustment heuristic is the tendency to be influenced by an initial value or anchor and then adjust final judgments based on it.

questions.prompt: In what kind of situations can automatic or gut-based processes lead to better decisions?
answers: Only when decisions are simple and involve very little information
When decisions involve more information than conscious thought can easily handle
Only when there is no time pressure at all
Only when people have explicit rules to follow
questions.explanation: Automatic processes can be advantageous when decisions involve many attributes or more information than conscious thought can easily process.

questions.prompt: What happened when people made a thoughtful list of all the reasons their relationship was good or bad?
answers: It became a better predictor than the gut rating
It had no predictive value at all
It improved memory for the relationship
It made people more satisfied with the relationship
questions.explanation: Making a thoughtful list of reasons for or against the relationship had no predictive value.

questions.prompt: What are Bargh's four horsemen of automaticity?
answers: Attention memory salience and priming
Intention control awareness and efficiency
Accuracy speed emotion and effort
Schemas stereotypes attitudes and norms
questions.explanation: The four horsemen of automaticity are intention, control, awareness, and efficiency.

questions.prompt: How many of the four horsemen does a process need to qualify as automatic?
answers: All four
Three of the four
Two of the four
Just one
questions.explanation: A process does not need all four horsemen to be considered automatic; one is enough. So instead of thinking of processes are automatic versus not automatic. Think of them as, this process is automatic in the sense that it is (unintentional or efficient or outside awareness or cannot be stopped)

questions.prompt: What does intention refer to in the four horsemen of automaticity?
answers: Whether you can stop a process once it starts
Whether you are consciously aware of a process
Whether you have the ability to start a process
Whether the process can occur while distracted
questions.explanation: Intention is whether you have the ability to start or initiate a process.

questions.prompt: What does control refer to in the four horsemen of automaticity?
answers: Whether you can stop a process
Whether you can remember a process
Whether a process is physiologically measurable
Whether a process takes place in the brain
questions.explanation: Control is whether you can stop a process once it has started.

questions.prompt: What does awareness refer to in the four horsemen of automaticity?
answers: Whether you are consciously aware of the process
Whether the process is accurate
Whether the process involves language
Whether the process requires priming
questions.explanation: Awareness is about whether you are consciously aware of the process.

questions.prompt: What does efficiency refer to in the four horsemen of automaticity?
answers: Whether a process is socially desirable
Whether a process happens in the heart rather than the brain
How much mental resources a process requires and whether it can run while distracted
Whether a process is initiated intentionally
questions.explanation: Efficiency is how many mental resources a process requires and whether it can continue under distraction.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between fMRI and EEG?
answers: fMRI measures when something happens in the brain and EEG measures where it happens
fMRI measures where something happens in the brain and EEG measures when it happens
Both measure both where and when equally well
Neither can be used to study automatic processes
questions.explanation: fMRI is used to determine where something happens in the brain, while EEG is used to determine when something happens.

questions.prompt: What is nonverbal leakage?
answers: The intentional use of gestures to persuade someone
The unintentional transmission of information through nonverbal behaviors
A way of unconsciously correcting first impressions
A form of nonverbal priming
questions.explanation: Nonverbal leakage is the unintentional transmission of information via nonverbal behaviors. Imagine a job interview unintentionally transmitting their dislike of minorities through nonverbal behavior like how close they sit to the job candidate.

questions.prompt: What is a self-fulfilling prophecy?
answers: A situation in which positive thinking magically changes reality
A process where expectations leak out and lead others to behave in ways that confirm those expectations
A tendency to ignore situational factors when making attributions
A deliberate attempt to discriminate against out-group members
questions.explanation: Expectations can leak out through behavior and cause others to respond in ways that make the original expectation come true.

questions.prompt: What did Word Zanna and Cooper find about white interviewers' behavior toward white versus Black job candidates?
answers: Interviewers treated both groups identically on all nonverbal behaviors
Interviewers were more immediate with white candidates than Black candidates
Interviewers spent more time with Black candidates than white candidates
Interviewers made more eye contact with Black candidates than white candidates
questions.explanation: White interviewers sat closer, made more eye contact, spent more time, and made fewer speech errors with white candidates, showing greater immediacy.

questions.prompt: Word, Zanna, and Cooper (1974) found that White participants who were interviewed by other Whites in a less immediate fashion (and in a style that was demonstrated in an earlier study with Blacks):
answers: sat closer to the interviewer
were rated as better applicants
showed less intelligence on a skill testing question
reciprocated the immediacy behaviors of the interviewer
questions.explanation: People of all races respond poorly when treated poorly

questions.prompt: Why can researchers not ask people directly about sensitive topics like race or sex?
answers: Because sensitive topics like race and sex are complex
Because people may not want to tell the truth or may not know the truth
Because direct questions are illegal on sensitive topics
Because sensitive topics can only be studied with neuroscience
questions.explanation: Direct questioning can fail on sensitive topics because people may not want to tell the truth or may not know the truth.

questions.prompt: What is the logic behind the Modern Racism Scale?
answers: It asks about racial attitudes while giving participants plausible non-racist reasons for negative responses
It measures attitudes by timing responses to racial words
It asks participants only about historical racism
It asks about race but does so in a way that promotes unity
questions.explanation: The Modern Racism Scale asks about attitudes toward Black people while providing plausible, non-racist reasons for expressing negative attitudes.

questions.prompt: What is the logic behind asking participants what they think society believes instead of what they personally believe?
answers: People usually separate their own attitudes from society's views
People tend to assume others think very differently from them
People tend to project their own attitudes onto society
This strategy works only for positive attitudes
questions.explanation: People often assume that most people think like they do, so asking about society's views can reveal their own attitudes.

questions.prompt: What is the most common error in the shooter task?
answers: Failing to shoot an armed White target
Shooting an armed Black target
Shooting an unarmed Black target
Not responding at all to an unarmed White target
questions.explanation: Participants make the most errors when judging an unarmed Black target, meaning the most common mistake is to shoot an unarmed Black person.

questions.prompt: What is self-esteem?
answers: A stable personality trait related to confidence
An evaluation of the self
The number of positive relationships a person has
A measure of how popular a person is
questions.explanation: Self-esteem is an evaluation of the self, or an attitude toward the self.

questions.prompt: What does the name-letter effect suggest about the self?
answers: People dislike anything connected to themselves
People tend to like things linked to themselves
People prefer only letters that appear in common words
People prefer letters based only on frequency
questions.explanation: People tend to like the letters in their own name because they tend to like themselves and therefore like things linked to themselves.

questions.prompt: Which effect is a way to measure implicit self-esteem?
answers: The halo effect
The name-letter effect
The primacy effect
The Stroop effect
questions.explanation: The name-letter effect is one example of how researchers measure implicit self-esteem.

questions.prompt: What pattern of responses would be expected from someone with high self-esteem on the IAT?
answers: Slower responses when self and positive share a key than when self and negative share a key
Similar response times in both blocks
Faster responses when self and positive share a key than when self and negative share a key
Faster responses only for other and negative pairings
questions.explanation: A person with high self-esteem should respond faster when self and positive concepts share a key than when self and negative concepts share a key.

questions.prompt: What is the logic behind the self-esteem IAT?
answers: Self-esteem is measured by how many self-related words people can remember
Implicit self-esteem is reflected in how much faster people respond when self and positive share a key than when self and negative share a key
Implicit self-esteem depends only on how accurately people categorize positive words
The IAT measures whether people consciously endorse positive self-beliefs
questions.explanation: The logic of the IAT is comparing how much faster participants respond when self and positive share a response key versus when self and negative share a response key.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to consider both implicit and explicit self-esteem?
answers: Because each measure captures exactly the same thing
Because together they can distinguish healthy high self-esteem from defensive high self-esteem
Because explicit self-esteem is always inaccurate on its own
Because implicit self-esteem only matters when someone is confused
questions.explanation: Considering implicit and explicit self-esteem together helps researchers tell more than either measure could on its own.

questions.prompt: Which best characterizes truly high self-esteem?
answers: High explicit self-esteem only
High implicit self-esteem only
High implicit and high explicit self-esteem together
Low implicit and high explicit self-esteem
questions.explanation: Truly high self-esteem requires both high implicit and high explicit self-esteem.

questions.prompt: Which best characterizes truly low self-esteem?
answers: Low explicit self-esteem only
Low implicit and low explicit self-esteem together
High implicit and low explicit self-esteem
Low implicit self-esteem only
questions.explanation: True low self-esteem requires both low implicit and low explicit self-esteem.

questions.prompt: What term describes people with high explicit self-esteem but low implicit self-esteem?
answers: Secure self-esteem
Defensive self-esteem
Social self-esteem
Stable self-esteem
questions.explanation: A person with high explicit but low implicit self-esteem is best described as having defensive self-esteem.

questions.prompt: What is affective forecasting?
answers: Predicting what events will happen in the future
Predicting how we will feel in response to future events
Remembering how we felt during past events
Changing our emotions after a difficult experience
questions.explanation: Affective forecasting is predicting how we will feel in response to events in the future.

questions.prompt: What aspect of future emotion do people usually predict correctly?
answers: The duration of the emotion
The exact behavior they will show
The type of emotion
The intensity of the emotion
questions.explanation: People generally know whether they will feel good or bad, meaning they are relatively accurate about the type of emotion but wrong about the duration and intensity of the emotion.

questions.prompt: What are attitudes?
answers: Stable personality traits
Evaluations of a person object or idea
Automatic memories about other people
Rules for making attributions
questions.explanation: Attitudes are an evaluation of a person, object, or idea.

questions.prompt: Why did Allport and other researchers consider attitudes indispensable?
answers: Because attitudes are stronger than attributions
Because attitudes predict behavior
Because attitudes are fundamental to the self
Because attitudes evolve over time
questions.explanation: Attitudes are indispensable because measuring them is often thought to be a great way to predict someone's behavior.

questions.prompt: What are the three components in the tripartite theory of attitudes?
answers: Behavior cognitions and affect
Approach avoidance and evaluation
Implicit explicit and behavioral tendencies
evaluations, attributions, and stereotypes
questions.explanation: The tripartite theory of attitudes argues that behavior, cognitions, and affect are components of attitudes.

questions.prompt: According to Eagly and Chaikin what components do attitudes consist of?
answers: An evaluative and a behavioral component
An evaluative component and an attribution component
A behavioral component and a stereotype compondent
A cognitive and physiological component
questions.explanation: Eagly and Chaikin argued that attitudes consist of an evaluative and a behavioral component.

questions.prompt: What did Thurstone argue about attitudes in 1928?
answers: Attitudes should only be studied through behavior
Attitudes could be measured by asking people their opinions or evaluations of people objects or ideas
Attitudes are too complex to measure directly
Attitudes are identical to stereotypes
questions.explanation: Thurstone argued that attitudes could be measured by asking people for their opinions or evaluations of people, objects, or ideas.

questions.prompt: What did LaPiere's 1934 study show us about the relation between attitudes and behavior?
answers: Hotel proprietors' stated attitudes always matched their behavior
Most hotel proprietors refused service to a visiting Chinese couple
Strongly expressed negative attitudes did not necessarily predict actual behavior
Chinese customers were refused at exactly the rate expected from survey responses
questions.explanation: Although many hotel proprietors reported they would refuse service to Chinese customers, almost none actually did so, showing a weak attitude-behavior link.

questions.prompt: What conclusion should we draw from Wicker's 1969 meta-analysis?
answers: Attitudes are always strong predictors of behavior
There was little evidence that attitudes were strong predictors of behavior
Behavior is irrelevant to attitudes
Implicit attitudes are weaker than explicit attitudes in every context
questions.explanation: Wicker's meta-analysis found little evidence that attitudes were strong predictors of behavior.

questions.prompt: What is a feeling thermometer?
answers: A task where people categorize positive and negative words
A scale where people indicate how warm or favorable they feel toward an attitude object
A measure of physiological arousal toward a target
A list of reasons for and against an attitude object
questions.explanation: A feeling thermometer asks people how warm or favorable they feel toward a person, object, or idea.

questions.prompt: What is a semantic differential?
answers: A scale using two opposing words such as good versus bad
A reaction time task measuring accessibility
A question asking participants to estimate social consensus
A measure of nonverbal behavior during interviews
questions.explanation: A semantic differential scale uses two opposing words, such as good-bad or pleasant-unpleasant.

questions.prompt: What is the logic behind using Modern Racism Scale-style questions?
answers: They directly ask whether a person dislikes a group
They provide a socially acceptable non-racist reason for expressing a negative attitude
They measure only explicit warmth toward a group
They avoid asking about social groups entirely

questions.prompt: What is the best predictor of planned behavior?

Behavioral intentions
Social norms alone
Behavioral control alone

questions.prompt: What are behavioral intentions?
answers: Automatic evaluations of an object
Conscious decisions to carry out specific actions
Unconscious habits that bypass thought
Stable personality traits
questions.explanation: Behavioral intentions are conscious decisions that carry out specific actions.

questions.prompt: What are general attitudes better at predicting?
answers: A single behavior in one immediate situation
A wide array of related behaviors over time
Only behaviors involving strong emotion
Only deliberate choices involving recycling
questions.explanation: General attitudes are better predictors of aggregate behavior across many related actions over time.

Political preferences
Alcohol and drug use
Personality traits

questions.prompt: What does MODE stand for?
answers: Motivation and Opportunity as Determinants of Whether Evaluation is explicit
Motivation and Opportunity as Determinants of Whether Behavior is Related to Spontaneous or Deliberate Processes
Measurement of Deliberate and Explicit Processes
Models of Deliberation and Emotion
questions.explanation: MODE is Motivation and Opportunity as Determinants of Whether Behavior is Related to Spontaneous or Deliberate Processes.

questions.prompt: According to the MODE model when should controlled processes be better predictors of behavior?

When behavior is highly deliberate and planned

Only for political attitudes

questions.prompt: What is the principle of self-presentation mainly concerned with?

How people manage the attitudes they report in order to avoid looking hypocritical in front of others
Changing unconscious attitudes through repetition
Predicting nonverbal behavior from implicit attitudes
questions.explanation: Self-presentation is a process in which people publicly report attitudes in ways that help them avoid looking like hypocrites.

Only when people are consciously aware of a possible discrepancy between their behavior and their attitude

questions.prompt: What is cognitive dissonance?
answers: A positive feeling that comes from agreeing with others
A tension that arises when two cognitions are inconsistent with each other
A habit of overthinking small decisions
A memory bias caused by priming
questions.explanation: Cognitive dissonance is a tension that arises from knowing that two of your cognitions are inconsistent with each other.

questions.prompt: Why did participants paid $20 experience relatively little dissonance in the boring experiment?

answers: Changing attitudes when there is not a strong justification for behavior that conflicts with those attitudes
Changing behavior to match social norms
Choosing the easiest option between two alternatives
Forgetting the reasons for a decision
questions.explanation: Insufficient justification means we change our attitudes to be more consistent with our behavior when we do not have a strong justification for acting contrary to our attitudes.

questions.prompt: What is post-decisional dissonance?

Feeling tension only when someone lies for money
Forgetting why a decision was made
Choosing between two options without any emotional reaction
questions.explanation: Post-decisional dissonance is attitude change after a choice, where chosen items become more attractive and rejected items less attractive.
